title: Optimally Assign Jobs to Workers under Resource Constraints with Overlapping Durations

statement.markdown: You are given a list of jobs, each represented by a tuple of (duration, resource requirement). The jobs have overlapping durations and each worker has a limited capacity. The goal is to assign the jobs to the workers in a way that maximizes the total number of assigned jobs. However, the current implementation has a bug that doesn't consider the order in which jobs are assigned to workers.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be provided as a list of jobs, where each job is a list of two integers, [duration, resource_requirement].
statement.outputFormat: The output should be the maximum number of jobs that can be assigned to the workers.
statement.notes: You can assume that there are enough workers to handle the jobs.

examples.explanation: We can assign all jobs to the first worker

hints.text: Sort the jobs based on their resource requirements
Use a greedy approach to assign jobs to workers

editorial.title: Optimal Solution
editorial.intuition: We need to assign the jobs to workers in a way that maximizes the total number of assigned jobs.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible assignments of jobs to workers and keeping track of the maximum number of jobs that can be assigned.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution involves sorting the jobs based on their resource requirements and then using a greedy approach to assign jobs to workers.
editorial.optimalSolution: In the optimal solution, we sort the jobs based on their resource requirements and then use a greedy approach to assign jobs to workers. We start by assigning the first job to the first worker and then continue assigning jobs to workers until we have assigned all jobs or we have run out of workers.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that the greedy approach always results in the assignment of the maximum number of jobs.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution involves using a sample input to see how the solution works.
commonMistakes: Not sorting the jobs based on their resource requirements
Not using a greedy approach to assign jobs to workers

functionSignature.python: def job_assigner(self, jobs: list[list[int]], num_workers: int) -> int:

testCases.input: First line count, second line elements (stdin match)
